name: Sarutahiko Ōkami
shortDescription: Leader of the earthly kami, he guided Ninigi's descent from the heavens, known for his imposing stature and role as a crossroads deity.
description: Sarutahiko Ōkami is a prominent figure in Japanese mythology, primarily known for his role in guiding Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu, during his descent from Takamagahara (the High Celestial Plain) to Ashihara no Nakatsukuni (the Central Land of Reed Plains, or the earthly realm). He is depicted as a towering figure with a large nose, ruddy face, and sparkling eyes, exuding an air of both power and benevolence. As the leader of the earthly kami, Sarutahiko's consent was crucial for Ninigi's peaceful arrival and subsequent establishment of imperial rule. His story is primarily detailed in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the earliest chronicles of Japanese history and mythology. Beyond his role as a guide, Sarutahiko is also revered as a deity of crossroads, strength, and agriculture, embodying the connection between the celestial and terrestrial realms. His eventual demise, drowning while fishing for shellfish, adds a tragic layer to his legend, further solidifying his place in the rich tapestry of Japanese folklore.
subtitle: The Guiding Kami of Crossroads and Power
achievements: Establishing peaceful relations between celestial and earthly kami
Becoming a deity of crossroads and safe passage
quests: Guiding Ninigi's descent and ensuring his safe arrival
Negotiating with Ame-no-Uzume to allow Ninigi passage
Overseeing the establishment of imperial rule on earth

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Sarutahiko Ōkami's origins are somewhat shrouded in mystery, with limited details provided about his parentage or early life in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. He appears as a powerful and imposing figure awaiting Ninigi's descent. His most significant role is his encounter with Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto, the goddess of dawn and revelry, who was sent ahead of Ninigi to negotiate with him. Initially intimidating, Sarutahiko was charmed by Uzume's bold and captivating dance, ultimately agreeing to guide Ninigi. This encounter is a pivotal moment, symbolizing the negotiation between the heavenly and earthly realms. Sarutahiko's later demise, drowning while fishing, is a poignant detail, reminding of the impermanence even of powerful kami.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Sarutahiko Ōkami holds a significant role in Japanese Shinto beliefs, primarily as a deity of crossroads, safe passage, and guidance. His guidance of Ninigi underscores his importance in the establishment of imperial rule and the connection between the celestial and terrestrial realms. He is often worshipped at crossroads and intersections, where travelers seek his protection and guidance. Additionally, he is associated with strength and agricultural prosperity, reflecting his earthly nature and connection to the land. Sarutahiko's influence extends to various aspects of Japanese culture, from religious practices to artistic representations, solidifying his position as a revered and influential kami.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Sarutahiko Ōkami's symbolism is rich and multifaceted. His imposing physical appearance, particularly his large nose and sparkling eyes, represents his power, wisdom, and ability to see clearly. As a deity of crossroads, he symbolizes choices, transitions, and the meeting of different paths. His role as a guide signifies leadership, protection, and the ability to navigate life's challenges. The act of Ame-no-Uzume charming Sarutahiko can be interpreted as the power of diplomacy and negotiation in overcoming obstacles. Overall, Sarutahiko represents the strength, guidance, and protection needed to navigate the complexities of life and the journey towards one's destiny.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Sarutahiko Ōkami's legacy continues to resonate in modern Japanese culture. He remains a popular deity worshipped at numerous shrines across the country, particularly those dedicated to safe travel and crossroads. His image is often depicted in art, literature, and popular media, reinforcing his iconic status. Sarutahiko's story is also used in traditional performances and festivals, keeping his legend alive for future generations. His association with strength, guidance, and crossroads makes him a relevant figure even in contemporary society, as people continue to seek his blessings for protection and success in their endeavors. The Tsubaki Grand Shrine is one of the most well known shrines dedicated to him.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, providing detailed accounts of Sarutahiko's role in Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, offering another perspective on Sarutahiko's significance.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: As a leader of earthly kami, he is closely associated with the land.

associations.name: Crossroads
associations.significance: He is the deity of crossroads, representing choices and transitions.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Hermes/Mercury
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are deities associated with guidance, travel, and crossroads.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Janus
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: God of beginnings, transitions, doorways, and endings, like Sarutahiko is associated with crossroads.

companions.name: Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto
companions.description: The goddess of dawn and revelry who negotiated with Sarutahiko and later became his consort.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of rice, sake, and other goods are made at shrines dedicated to Sarutahiko.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers for Safe Travel
worshipPractices.description: Travelers often pray to Sarutahiko for protection and guidance on their journeys.

festivals.name: Various Local Festivals
festivals.description: Many local festivals celebrate Sarutahiko, often involving processions and performances.

cultural.modernLegacy: Sarutahiko remains a popular deity, with shrines dedicated to him found throughout Japan. He is often invoked for safe travel, success in business, and general good fortune. His image is also used in art and popular culture.
